item.title: Dead fish found on Duke of York, ENB
item.description: DEAD fish sightings were made in East New Britain’s Duke of York last week with authorities closely monitoring and assessing the situation. Authorities received reports that dead fish had washed ashore at Molot on May 10, as well as in three other wards – Urkuk, Palipal and Kalibomo. However, only Molot and Urkuk had physical evidence of dead fish.
item.content:encoded:
DEAD fish sightings were made in East New Britain’s Duke of York last week with authorities closely monitoring and assessing the situation.

Authorities received reports that dead fish had washed ashore at Molot on May 10, as well as in three other wards – Urkuk, Palipal and Kalibomo. However, only Molot and Urkuk had physical evidence of dead fish.

The fish deaths occurred in a single wave and raised concerns among communities in the affected areas.

The National has been reporting on similar incidents along the eastern coastline of New Ireland province at Kafkaf and on Manus province.

This is the first sighting in East New Britain.

The matter was reported to the Provincial Fisheries Division and the Provincial Disaster Office and word is that the Local Level Government (LLG) administration had been tasked to conduct a preliminary assessment of the issue.

DOY LLG Manager Andrew Karubim confirmed that the LLG’s Environmental Health Officer had been assigned to carry out an assessment and submit a report to the Provincial Disaster Office.

“We are still monitoring the situation,” he said. “I assigned the environmental officer to conduct an assessment and report to the relevant authorities.”

Karubim said the assessment was still ongoing, with reports expected to be released soon.

Restrictions have been imposed to prevent people from harvesting or consuming fish from the affected areas until authorities complete their assessment.

No reports of skin infections or other health-related effects have been received so far.

“Only the fish is dying,” Karubim confirmed.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Airport closed temporarily after plane bursts tyres
item.description: THE Goroka Airport in Eastern Highlands was temporarily closed yesterday following an incident in which an aircraft burst its tyres while trying to avoid birds crossing the runway. The incident happened during take-off around 10am.
item.content:encoded:
THE Goroka Airport in Eastern Highlands was temporarily closed yesterday following an incident in which an aircraft burst its tyres while trying to avoid birds crossing the runway.

The incident happened during take-off around 10am.

National Airports Corporation (NAC) chief operating officer Manuai Kametan, said the 32 passengers and crew members were safe. 

“A Notice to Airmen was issued at 10.59am to close the runway while PNG Air, in coordination with the NAC, carried out inspections and repairs to tow the aircraft from the runway,” he said.

According to a statement from PNG Air, the ATR 72-600 aircraft was travelling at about 70 knots when the pilot rejected take off.

“The pilot acted in strict compliance with Casa procedures, mitigating the risk of bird ingestion into the engines, which could have caused significant damage or an in-flight shutdown.”

The statement said the aircraft remained secured on the runway under the supervision of PNG Air and airport authorities.

A rescue team has started recovery operations.

The aircraft is expected to return to service today.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Morobe disaster team ‘monitoring’ weather conditions
item.description: MOROBE disaster coordinator Charlie Masange says the disaster centre team is monitoring weather conditions in the province after unstable patterns were experienced recently. Masange told The National that the team would focus on communities with no access by road or boat, in case a natural disaster happened.
item.content:encoded:
MOROBE disaster coordinator Charlie Masange says the disaster centre team is monitoring weather conditions in the province after unstable patterns were experienced recently.

Masange told The National that the team would focus on communities with no access by road or boat, in case a natural disaster happened.

“Our focus is on places that do not have accessibility,” he said.

“Those that can be reached through vehicle and boat are to be assisted after.”

With warnings of El Nino conditions setting in, Masange said that the provincial disaster coordination office was yet to receive disaster reports from Finschhafen, Tewae-Siassi, Kabwum, Markham, Bulolo, Lae, Nawaeb and Menyamya.

“The Huon Gulf district development authority has already prepared a disaster relief assistance budget and requested further assistance from the disaster office following the recent disaster at the Morobe Patrol-Post,” he said.

“We cannot do much on our own because we don’t have enough funding, therefore, we would need assistance from local level governments (LLG) and districts.”

Masange said the National Weather Service had issued a letter previously about a country-wide drought.

“We have experienced heavy rainfall in recent weeks that has affected some districts, such as the flooding in Wau/Waria and towards the coastal villages of Huon Gulf in the past months,” he said.

Masange said districts such as Finschhafen and areas towards Tewae-Siassi in Sialum were prone to dry seasons and bush fire.

“Siassi Island and Wasu have grassland areas that might be affected by a drought or El Nino disaster but there is no official warning given,” he said.

He added that for Lae, and all the other nine districts, no warning had been issued but precautions were being taken.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Department clarifies stop-work at sections of Kokopo-Tokua corridor
item.description: THE Department of Works and Highways has clarified misinformation regarding the incomplete construction of the East New Britain’s Tokua-Kokopo four-lane road. In a statement to ENB governor Michael Marum, the department confirmed that work had not been halted and the Tokua-Kokopo four-lane road construction was progressing to schedule.
item.content:encoded:
THE Department of Works and Highways has clarified misinformation regarding the incomplete construction of the East New Britain’s Tokua-Kokopo four-lane road.

In a statement to ENB governor Michael Marum, the department confirmed that work had not been halted and the Tokua-Kokopo four-lane road construction was progressing to schedule.

The statement also confirmed that the national government, through the department under its Connect PNG Programme, was meeting payments to the contractor Covec PNG Limited, and there were no issues with the project.

The National obtained an update yesterday on the progress of road works from the department’s New Guinea Islands regional office in Kokopo.

According to provincial Works manager Bill Lawrie, road work is continuing.

However, construction has been paused in areas affected by land disputes along the road corridor, while work in other sections is still ongoing.

Lawrie said there were only three disputed areas along the road corridor, the Ulaveo Plantation section, the Kabakaul to Rapopo section under the Rataumama Land Group and the Seaview section under the Catholic Arch Diocese of Rabaul.

“We are working with the ENB provincial administration to sort out the issues,” he said.

“Payment has already been made for the Kabakaul-Rapopo section, while a meeting between provincial Works, the administration, the Catholic Diocese of Rabaul and Ulaveo Plantation is scheduled for tomorrow (today) to resolve matters for the Ulaveo and Seaview sections.

“Once sorted with the landowners, work will commence in those areas.”

The landowners for these three disputed sections are seeking assurance that their concerns will be addressed now that the road will pass through their land.

The provincial administration has confirmed it will sort out the issues.

Lawrie said work had now started on a kilometre of road sealing from Tokua Airport.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: AJ’s 10 straight shots. Chiefs 002 cements legendary status, betters Blake mark
item.description: ALEX Johnston has now equaled Phil Blake’s 1983 record of scoring tries in 10 straight matches, further cementing his place among rugby league’s greatest finishers in the National Rugby League (NRL). “It’s pretty cool getting tries in 10 games in a row,” Johnston said.
item.content:encoded:
ALEX Johnston has now equaled Phil Blake’s 1983 record of scoring tries in 10 straight matches, further cementing his place among rugby league’s greatest finishers in the National Rugby League (NRL).

“It’s pretty cool getting tries in 10 games in a row,” Johnston said.

“But every time I throw in a game now, I get messages saying I broke the all-time record again. That’s pretty cool too, but I’m just trying to do my job for the team.”

The NRL all-time leading try scorer is currently in Papua New Guinea alongside teammates Sean Keppie and Peter Mamouzelos as part of a community visit.

The Rabbitohs flyer admitted South Sydney’s season had been inconsistent so far, but he hopes his strong try-scoring form can help lift the side in the coming weeks.

“We’ve had a bit of an up-and-down season, so hopefully I can keep those tries coming,” he said.

While Keppie has visited Papua New Guinea before, Johnston said it was important for Mamouzelos to experience the country and its culture for the first time.

“I like bringing the boys across to experience the culture and see what PNG is really about,” Johnston said.

“They get to meet the people, see the land and give back to the community. I think they’re excited to be here.”

The PNG Kumul No.294 also brushed aside ongoing concerns about safety in Papua New Guinea, saying he had only ever experienced warmth and respect from the people during his visits.

“Safety’s not my concern at all,” he said.

“Every time I come here, everyone is welcoming.

“People just want photos, signatures and to show support.

“You’ve got to come and see for yourself. Once players come here and experience it, they realise safety is not really a concern for us.”

Johnston also spoke emotionally about his pride in his Papua New Guinean heritage and the entry of the PNG Chiefs in 2028.

As the first player of Papua New Guinea heritage to achieve so much in the NRL, Johnston said the moment meant a lot to his family.

“My family was over the moon,” he said. “My mum was tearing up and saying if only my grandmother was alive to see it.

Johnston said he was already looking forward to the historic arrival of the PNG franchise.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Bears launch foundation membership
item.description: PERTH: The Perth Bears Rugby League Club today announced an extraordinary response to the launch of its foundation memberships – with more than 16,000 fans and counting signed up in just a few days. Perth Bears boss Anthony De Ceglie said 85 per cent of the memberships hailed from Western Australia – proving the State was truly ready to put the “national” in National Rugby League.
item.content:encoded:
PERTH: The Perth Bears Rugby League Club today announced an extraordinary response to the launch of its foundation memberships – with more than 16,000 fans and counting signed up in just a few days.

Perth Bears boss Anthony De Ceglie said 85 per cent of the memberships hailed from Western Australia – proving the State was truly ready to put the “national” in National Rugby League.

“We had set a target of 10,000 foundation members by the end of October,” De Ceglie. “We beat that number in the first 24-hours with at least triple the demand on Day One alone.

“By the end of our first weekend of sales we’re at an incredible 16,000 members with a new target of hitting 20,000 in our sights.

“We are truly grateful and honoured by all the support from our fanbase in Western Australian and in North Sydney thanks to the legacy and tradition of the North Sydney Bears.

“This proves that the Perth Bears will be a powerhouse of not just the NRL but of the entire national sporting landscape. And it proves that our coast-to-coast story is one that has been embraced.”

The foundation memberships will give fans access to tickets and season memberships as they go on sale later this year. – NRL.com

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Dabaris, Tigers lead NYC pools
item.description: THE National Youth Competition (NYC) Under-19 rugby league competition is heating up after six rounds, with Central Dabaris and Lae Snax Tigers leading their respective pools. The Dabaris lead Pool A with five wins from six matches.
item.content:encoded:
THE National Youth Competition (NYC) Under-19 rugby league competition is heating up after six rounds, with Central Dabaris and Lae Snax Tigers leading their respective pools.

The Dabaris lead Pool A with five wins from six matches.

Their recent win over the ENB Gurias 22-10 on Sunday takes them 10 competition points with a positive points differential of 38.

In second place are the Port Moresby Vipers with seven points from three wins, one draw and two losses.

Vipers thrashed Gulf Isou 42-4 on Sunday.

The Vipers have been one of the competition’s strongest attacking teams, recording an impressive points differential of 52 – the best in Pool A. The ENB Gurias Under-19 team are holding third place with six points after recording two wins, two draws and two losses.

At the bottom of Pool A are the Isou, who are still searching for their first win of the season.

The Isou has recorded a draw and five losses, while their points differential of minus 94 highlights their defensive struggles.

Over in Pool B, the Lae Tigers have stamped their authority on the competition. Tigers beat Mt Hagen Eagles 14-4.

The Tigers lead the Momase and Highlands region with five wins from six matches and sit on 10 points.

Their balanced performances in both attack and defence have helped them maintain a healthy points differential of 28.

The Eagles sit in second place with three wins and three losses.

The Goroka Lahanis are in third place with four competition points after winning two matches and losing four since the begining of the season.

They lost to the Enga Mioks 4-6 over the weekend.

The Lahanis have struggled to find consistency this season, reflected in their negative points differential of minus 16.

Sharing the same points as the Lahanis are the Mioks, who sit fourth in Pool B.

The Mioks have also recorded two wins and four losses, but their points differential of minus 20 places them below the Lahanis on the ladder.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: New mindset helps Goroka Lahanis upset Vipers
item.description: THE Bintagor Goroka Lahanis finally ended their losing streak after producing a tough 18-12 victory over an in-form Moni Plus Port Moresby Vipers on Sunday at the Sir John Guise Stadium in Port Moresby. The important win came after two disappointing weeks for the Lahanis, who suffered a narrow 26-24 loss to the Asila Waghi Tumbe in round six before being heavily beaten, 36-12, by the New Porgera L
item.content:encoded:
THE Bintagor Goroka Lahanis finally ended their losing streak after producing a tough 18-12 victory over an in-form Moni Plus Port Moresby Vipers on Sunday at the Sir John Guise Stadium in Port Moresby.

The important win came after two disappointing weeks for the Lahanis, who suffered a narrow 26-24 loss to the Asila Waghi Tumbe in round six before being heavily beaten, 36-12, by the New Porgera Ltd Enga Mioks in round five.

According to head coach Michael Moses, a complete change in mindset helped inspire the players to bounce back strongly against the capital city side.

Moses said he challenged his players before the match to stop thinking about simply enjoying rugby league and instead focus on fighting for their families during difficult economic times.

The coach revealed that after last week’s loss, he reminded the team to play for the Goroka flag and represent their province with pride, but they still fell short.

Ahead of Sunday’s clash, Moses changed his message completely.

He told the players that life was becoming harder for everyone and they needed to treat the game like work, where effort and sacrifice would help them earn a living and support their families.

The new approach worked as the Lahanis defended strongly for long periods and frustrated the dangerous Vipers attack.

The coach compared the team’s struggle to people standing beside traffic lights selling water just to survive, saying many players were also facing personal and financial challenges at home.

He said those emotional words helped motivate the squad and pushed them to work harder for each other.

The victory now gives the Lahanis confidence as they prepare to host ladder leaders Madang Kandes this weekend in round 8 at their home ground, the National Sports Institute in Goroka, Eastern Highlands.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Rising costs, failing governance
item.description: AS children, many Papua New Guineans remember a time when the Kina had strong buying power. Even K2 could provide for basic needs, families could comfortably afford food, clothing, school fees, and healthcare.
item.content:encoded:
AS children, many Papua New Guineans remember a time when the Kina had strong buying power.

Even K2 could provide for basic needs, families could comfortably afford food, clothing, school fees, and healthcare.

Poverty levels were lower, employment opportunities were better, and both formal and informal sectors operated with stability and honesty.

Over time, however, poor economic planning, continuous deficit budgets, and increasing dependence on foreign loans weakened the nation’s economy.

Instead of building sustainable systems, governments relied on offshore assistance while inflation, unemployment, and the cost of living continued to rise.

Public concerns also grew over the management and reform of key institutions such as Telikom, PNG Power, and Kumul Consolidated Holdings.

Today, Papua New Guinea faces rising debt, unemployment, crime, poor healthcare, and declining investor confidence.

Many citizens believe stronger governance, accountability, and sustainable economic reforms are urgently needed.

The key question is whether the State should strengthen, regulate, privatise, or expand major industries and institutions to create jobs, improve services, and restore long-term economic stability.

A RECENT article published by a multilateral organisation paints a grim picture of Papua New Guinea’s future, warning that the country is heading towards political, economic and social decline.

PNG remains one of the most expensive countries in which to conduct public and private business, while poor leadership, weak management and ineffective governance continue to undermine development.

Despite its vast natural resources, including minerals, oil, gas, timber and tuna, the country has struggled to translate wealth into meaningful economic and social progress.

A lack of transparency and accountability in public spending and resource management has weakened the link between resource revenues and national development.

As a result, many Papua New Guineans continue to face deteriorating living conditions.

The country is grappling with high costs of government services through State-owned enterprises, declining health and education standards, poor transport infrastructure, rising unemployment, and worsening law and order issues.

Social problems, including widespread drug and alcohol abuse, are also increasing, while environmental damage caused by logging and mining continue to grow.

Smoking and betel nut chewing have further contributed to a growing public health crisis.

The article argues that PNG must reform its natural resource-licencing system by adopting a production-sharing arrangement regime.

Such a system would allow the country greater ownership and control over its resources while increasing revenues for national development.

It also calls on citizens to elect new leaders in General Election 2027, capable of steering the country towards long-term growth and prosperity.

RECENT online commentary has pointed to shifting political and economic alignments in parts of Africa, including Libya, Niger and Sudan, with suggestions that some countries are reassessing relationships with Western partners.

These claims are widely debated and not consistently verified, but they reflect broader discussions about global power shifts and development models.

Overall, these discussions highlight ongoing geopolitical tensions rather than clear, confirmed policy shifts.

For Papua New Guinea, the takeaway is the importance of carefully evaluating foreign partnerships, whether Western, Asian or otherwise.

Sustainable development depends on strong governance, transparency, and building domestic capacity, particularly in sectors like renewable energy and infrastructure.

Rather than relying solely on external narratives, PNG’s focus should remain on evidence-based policy, diversified investment, and long-term national resilience.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Childbirth on floor without dignity
item.description: FOR months, women at Port Moresby General Hospital (PMGH) have been delivering on the floor, with more than 30 cases since February. These are mothers and babies facing childbirth without basic safety, privacy or dignity.
item.content:encoded:
FOR months, women at Port Moresby General Hospital (PMGH) have been delivering on the floor, with more than 30 cases since February.

These are mothers and babies facing childbirth without basic safety, privacy or dignity.

Dr Glen Mola has described the situation as “neither dignified nor safe”, highlighting a deeper system failure.

Pregnancy and childbirth are already high-risk, and the lack of beds and resources further endangers lives.

His advice for families in the National Capital District to reconsider pregnancy timing or use family planning reflects harm reduction in an overstretched system, not fear-mongering.

Midwives and doctors at Susu Mama are working beyond safe limits, managing overwhelming patient loads while continuing to provide care under extreme pressure.

PMGH, as the national referral hospital, is carrying a burden it was not designed for, without adequate beds, staff, or funding.

Without stronger provincial and district health services, the pressure will remain.

PNG already faces high maternal mortality, and overcrowding increases risks of infection and complications.

Immediate action is needed through emergency support for PMGH and long-term investment in maternity services nationwide.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Weak law enforcement failing residents
item.description: THERE is increasing concern that the vagrancy laws passed recently are not being effectively enforced in Port Moresby. Many residents believe the lack of strict enforcement has allowed illegal street vending and the betel nut trade to continue throughout the city despite laws being passed to control these activities.
item.content:encoded:
THERE is increasing concern that the vagrancy laws passed recently are not being effectively enforced in Port Moresby.

Many residents believe the lack of strict enforcement has allowed illegal street vending and the betel nut trade to continue throughout the city despite laws being passed to control these activities.

For many law-abiding citizens, the vagrancy law was expected to improve public order, cleanliness, and safety within the nation’s capital.

However, when laws are passed, but not consistently enforced, people view them as weak or ineffective.

This encourages unlawful behaviour, when individuals see there are no consequences for breaking the law.

Police are the frontline enforcers of laws and policies. Strong, fair, and consistent law enforcement can restore public confidence and discourage illegal activities.

At the same time, enforcement must remain lawful and respectful of human rights while recognising the economic difficulties faced by many unemployed people and informal settlers.

Ultimately, long-term solutions will require not only stronger law enforcement, but also employment opportunities, urban planning, and social support programmes to address the root causes of street vending and vagrancy.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Diversify government board appointments
item.description: THERE is growing concern in Papua New Guinea about the repeated appointment of the same individuals to government boards and State-owned enterprises. Many believe the country has a broad pool of qualified Papua New Guinean professionals capable of serving in senior leadership positions, raising questions about fairness, transparency, and equal opportunity in public appointments.
item.content:encoded:
THERE is growing concern in Papua New Guinea about the repeated appointment of the same individuals to government boards and State-owned enterprises.

Many believe the country has a broad pool of qualified Papua New Guinean professionals capable of serving in senior leadership positions, raising questions about fairness, transparency, and equal opportunity in public appointments.

Institutions such as Kumul Petroleum Holdings Limited manage critical national assets and billions of Kina in public wealth.

As such, leadership appointments must uphold the highest standards of integrity, competence, and accountability.

This issue is not about any an individual, as all citizens are entitled to due process and the presumption of innocence.

However, public appointments should also strengthen public confidence in national institutions.

Papua New Guinea should consider stronger “fit and proper person” standards, independent vetting, and more transparent appointment processes.

Broader inclusion of qualified professionals would encourage leadership renewal, improve governance, and help restore public trust in key national institutions.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Pacific concerns about militarisation and NZ’s role in it
item.description: NEW Zealand’s government is increasingly eager to promote the buy-in of Pacific nations for closer defence force integration in the region, amid concerns about militarisation of the region. The security environment has been shifting rapidly, and regional defence is becoming more complex, leaving Pacific Islanders wondering if their “Ocean of Peace” is slipping out of their grasp.
item.content:encoded:
NEW Zealand’s government is increasingly eager to promote the buy-in of Pacific nations for closer defence force integration in the region, amid concerns about militarisation of the region.

The security environment has been shifting rapidly, and regional defence is becoming more complex, leaving Pacific Islanders wondering if their “Ocean of Peace” is slipping out of their grasp.

In recent months, the defence and police forces of Australia and New Zealand have been increasing cooperation with counterparts in Pacific countries, including Fiji, Tonga, Samoa and Vanuatu in efforts to combat transnational crime, especially the illicit drug trade. But as a number of Pacific Island countries weigh up signing major bilateral treaties or agreements with the likes of Australia, China and the United States (US), New Zealand has been steadily pushing Pacific regional defence cooperation on a number of fronts.

The communiqué from last October’s South Pacific Defence Ministers Meeting (SPDMM) in Chile is instructive.

The SPDMM – which involves New Zealand, Australia, Chile, Fiji, France, Papua New Guinea and Tonga – notes the leadership role New Zealand has taken on better coordinating regional defence architecture.

The member countries agreed to push for a defence advisor from their collective to be embedded in the secretariat of the Pacific Islands Forum (PIF), whose secretary-general Baron Waqa attended the Chile meeting and appeared to support closer integration.

While the advisor position is yet to be established, the SPDMM is surging ahead with a range of new regional defence initiatives, including developing the Pacific Response Group, under which defence personnel from Australia, Fiji, France, New Zealand and PNG work together to support coordinated humanitarian assistance and disaster relief responses across the Pacific.

A briefing from March’s joint meeting of the defence and foreign ministers of Australia and New Zealand, emphasised how they aim to promote “the sense of integration through Pacific defence forces’ and to ‘enhance the sense of Pacific forces meeting Pacific security needs”.

It also highlighted a keenness to “get more links between SPDMM and PIF so that these voices are heard directly by the region”.

“The briefings reveal New Zealand’s role in integrating and aligning Pacific defence forces alongside a considerable anxiety about regional social licence,” Pacific historian Marco de Jong said.

He said the language being used “speaks to a programme of influence and public relations, calibrated to downplay criticism that New Zealand is contributing to the militarisation of the Pacific”.

A representative with Development Alternatives with Women for a New Era, Maureen Penjueli, who is also a long time advocate for indigenous rights in the region, said there had been a lack of consultation with the wider Pacific Islands region about the new defence tack.

“We’ve seen so much occupation by those in the defence interests area. For example, the Australian National Security College takes a very primary seat at the Pacific Islands Forum on security. We’ve got competing interests, which is the Fusion Centre that’s in Vanuatu,” Penjueli said.

“When you add more and more players to this regional architecture that already has enough players on defence and security, it complicates the governance structure in a way.

“It does not go through the rigour of national consultations, consultations with civil society around some of these bigger significant shifts around defence and security.”

New Zealand is a long-standing contributor to Pacific regional initiatives, and its defence force is well valued in the region, especially in responding to disasters, humanitarian needs, transnational crime and maritime security threats, and also including in training support.

New Zealand’s defence minister Chris Penk, who replaced Judith Collins in the role since March’s 2+2 Ministerial Meeting with Australia, said that New Zealand always sought to adapt its work with Pacific partners to their context, culture and operational needs.

He told RNZ that in order to support closer cooperation between Pacific militaries, members were also looking at a SPDMM Status of Forces Agreement.

“This would provide a common legal framework for personnel to deploy into each other’s countries more easily, strengthening our collective ability to respond to maritime security challenges as well as humanitarian and disaster relief events.”

Asked if New Zealand is contributing to militarisation of the Pacific Islands, Penk said Pacific partners had warmly welcomed the country’s continued presence and partnership in the region

“The New Zealand defence force contributes to regional responses where it is agreed that defence force personnel and assets should be involved, including humanitarian assistance, maritime domain awareness, fisheries patrols, and search and rescue operations.

“As a Pacific military, we are proud to work alongside our Pacific partners to help respond to the challenges facing our region.”

Penjueli warned that militarisation of the region was escalating against the wishes of most Pacific Islands people.

Making things more complicated, she said, was the growing number of security treaties and agreements that Island countries were being drawn to.

She said they were no longer just about defence or security inter-operability, and often included development and economic dimensions, arrangements that “entangled” Pacific countries into wide ranging commitments beyond traditional military and security ties.

Penjueli worried that the interests of the Islands countries themselves were more than ever being buried under broader geopolitical jostling.

“We were told that this is to ready the region in an anticipation, to contain China, and we’re told that this is about the drug trade and the drug war that’s taking place.

“Yet for the Pacific, climate change or the climate crisis, remains our significant issue around security. So, I think the agendas are very different.”

At their last leaders summit, PIF countries signed up to the Blue Pacific Ocean of Peace Declaration, formally committing the region to peace, sovereignty, and climate justice.

However, Penjueli said being a true ocean of peace required demilitarisation and de-escalation – something which she suggested is not the direction that the defence-oriented governments of the region are heading in. – RNZ

item.title: Ex-security guard Zeming graduates with diploma
item.description: THANKS to young sister Penelope who had challenged family members to pursue further studies and graduate from tertiary institutions, Zeming Zeming received not only a Diploma in Business Management but also the top-student award at the International Training Institute graduation on Friday. “In my immediate family, only the two of us have graduated – me and my small sister (Penelope). She had
item.content:encoded:
THANKS to young sister Penelope who had challenged family members to pursue further studies and graduate from tertiary institutions, Zeming Zeming received not only a Diploma in Business Management but also the top-student award at the International Training Institute graduation on Friday.

“In my immediate family, only the two of us have graduated – me and my small sister (Penelope). She had graduated earlier (in 2022) and she used to challenge us to obtain qualifications (from tertiary institutions) as well.”

Zeming, 31, of Zankoa village in Tewae-Siassi district, Morobe used to score good marks in primary school but never became a top student or dux.

“I was never a dux. This is my first time being a dux. I thank God and my family for this.”

Zeming is the third eldest of five siblings – two brothers and three sisters. Their father Mao Zeming is self-employed while Mum Fredah died in 2018.

Zeming did not complete school because of financial issues. So he took on informal jobs to enroll in the diploma course at ITI.

“I didn’t complete my schooling due to school fee issues. I looked for jobs instead. I became a bus driver, security guard and all sorts of informal jobs until this opportunity arose and I grabbed it with both hands.”

His job as a security guard kindled his interest in management.

“I worked as a security and was happy to interact with people. I felt like I could manage their small complaints and issues. It got me interested in the management course.”

He faced challenges during the two years of study.

“The first two years were not easy. I tried my best to go to work and attend classes. And in the end, I achieved this.”

His family motivated him to get his diploma.

“We are a close-knit family. Every time my brother would counsel me. He reminded me of our humble beginnings and not to take education for granted. It was their motivation that kept me going.”

His father’s strict rules on time guided his study routine.

“ I didn’t complete my schooling due to school fee issues. I looked for jobs instead. I became a bus driver, security guard and all sorts of informal jobs until this opportunity arose and I grabbed it with both hands.”

“Focus is one thing that my father talks about every time, reminding us to be early before schedule and all that. He is a very strict man, very strict on time.

“Some things we don’t know about, he encourages us to ask people for help, to be curious. So yes, it was my father’s strictness that had shaped my study routine.”

“I always make sure that I did my homework and my studies and that things are in order.

“The good thing is that, I have a good family. They are very understanding people. Once in a while they would ask me for help. But mostly, they respect me; they played their part and mine as well.”

“My brother is always my role model. He used to remind us that success is there, all you need to do to achieve it is to remain focused.

“He does not have a big job but he ensures that we all are taken care of. He took on the family responsibility and I have respect for him.”

Zeming is happy to have accomplished his goal.

“I am really happy. Seeing (cousin sister) Luddy also graduating today makes me really happy. We helped each other at school. We also help other Morobe students as well and in the end, we made it.”

Zeming dedicated the dux award to his late mother and his diploma to his father.

“I dedicated these achievements to my family, those who had helped me with my school fees and my upbringing. And this dux award is dedicated to my late mum. Dad gets the diploma.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Neidab dedicates degree, award to late father
item.description: WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father. “I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father.

“I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”

Neidab lost his father Ulu Rusini last year during the second semester of his final year – a critical time for any final year university student.

However, he managed to pull through and graduated with a Bachelor in Public Finance and Accountancy degree.

Neidab, 39, from Wanigela Village, Abau District, Central, is the second eldest of six children.

He was among those who graduated from the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy last Thursday.

To his employer, the Public Service Commission, he was an investment which paid off when he not only graduated but also named the top performing student in the Bachelor of Public Finance and Accountancy class.

He received the Academic Excellence Award after being sponsored by the PSC, where he has worked for 11 years, to pursue the degree while working as the acting Senior Budget Officer and Certifying Officer.

It was an opportunity he took seriously.

“I thank my employer, the Public Service Commission, for the investment and opportunity and trust placed in me to send me for further studies.”

His work involves budget support, expenditure management, and financial advisory duties. He knows that the degree will help him in his work and lift his performance.

“What motivated me to go back for further studies was to enhance my skills in my current duties on the processes in place to manage the public funds effectively and efficiently.”

Neidab attained a Certificate in Business Studies in 2007 and a Diploma in Business Studies in 2008 at the Port Moresby Business College.

In 2023, he graduated with a Diploma in Accounting from the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (SILAG).

The loss of his father affected him. On top of that, he faced financial issues and tried his best to juggle his time between work, study, and family.

“I encountered financial challenges attending classes, attending to family matters, as well as work.”

At times when work and study demanded his attention most, he prioritised which one to tackle first.

“I’ve had clashes between my studies and my job. Some time, I had to attend to my work first and then my studies second because work is my bread and butter.”

The experience changed how he managed his time.

“It shaped me as a person to think critically and handle issues in a way that is balanced.”

At home, his wife Wendy Ulu and two sons Neidrolson who is in Grade Six, and Braden in Grade 4, endured nights when he had to burn the midnight oil.

Wife Wendy remembers those nights when they hardly went to sleep as Neidab would spend hours studying and keeping the lights on.

So she was happy and relieved to see him graduate with flying colours – a fitting reward for his hard work and perseverance. For the family, the hours of struggling to sleep are finally over.

Neidab dedicated the award to his employer, his late father, and his family.

“I dedicate the award to my employer and to my father who should have attended and witnessed this ceremony. And not forgetting my family who supported me throughout this journey.”
